title: The practice of choosing yourself after a lifetime of choosing everyone else
author: Kalesh

htmlContent:
The moment I realized I had disappeared

It happened in a therapist's office, three years ago. I was listing all the reasons I couldn't set a boundary with someone who was hurting me. My therapist leaned forward and said, "Kalesh, where are you in this sentence?"

I had no idea what she meant.

I thought about it. I had talked about what my partner needed, what my parents expected, what my friends wanted from me, what my boss demanded, what society said was right. I had talked about everyone except myself. I was describing a life where I existed to meet other people's needs. And I had been doing it for so long that I couldn't even see myself anymore.

Here's the thing. If you grew up in a household where your needs were ignored, punished, or treated as an inconvenience, you learned something very specific. You learned that your survival depended on making everyone else comfortable. You learned that your wants didn't matter. You learned that asking for anything was dangerous. You learned that being small and agreeable was the price of safety.

I spent thirty years paying that price. Every single day.

And I'm not alone in this. This is what happens when you grow up in a system that demands your selfhood as the entry fee for love. You don't just learn to put others first. You learn that putting yourself first is selfish, wrong, and possibly catastrophic. You learn that your needs are the problem. You learn that your very existence is something that needs to be managed and minimized.

But here's what nobody tells you. Choosing yourself after a lifetime of choosing everyone else is not a simple decision. It's not a single boundary you set and then you're done. It's not a bubble bath and a face mask. It's not a mantra you repeat in the mirror. It's a full-blown reclamation project that will take years and will shake the foundations of every relationship you have.

Stay with me here. I need you to understand that this is hard. This is the hardest thing you will ever do. And it is also the only way to actually live.

The nervous system's role in your disappearance

Stephen Porges, the researcher who developed Polyvagal Theory, would tell you that your nervous system learned to prioritize connection over your own well-being. This isn't a character flaw. It's a survival strategy. Your nervous system scanned your early environment and figured out that being in relationship with your caregivers was necessary for survival. So it made a deal with the devil. It said, "I will suppress my own needs, my own voice, my own boundaries, if it means I can stay connected and stay safe."

And that deal worked. For a while. It kept you alive. It kept you from being abandoned. It kept you from being hurt worse.

But here's the problem. Your nervous system doesn't know that you're not a child anymore. It doesn't know that you have resources now. It doesn't know that you can survive someone being angry at you. It doesn't know that you can survive being rejected. It still thinks that the only way to stay alive is to make yourself invisible.

So when you try to choose yourself, your nervous system will fight you. It will flood you with anxiety. It will make you feel like you're about to die. It will whisper that you're being selfish, that you're a bad person, that you're going to be abandoned and alone forever.

Wild, right? Your own body will become the enemy of your own liberation.

This is why I tell people that choosing yourself is not a mental exercise. You can't think your way into it. You have to work with your nervous system. You have to teach it, slowly and gently, that it's safe to have needs. That it's safe to say no. That it's safe to take up space.

One resource that often resonates deeply with C-PTSD survivors is Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link). Pete Walker's work on emotional flashbacks and the inner critic is a lifeline for anyone who feels like they're fighting their own brain every time they try to prioritize themselves.

But here's the thing. Reading the book is not the same as doing the work. The book will show you the map. You still have to walk the territory. And the territory is your own nervous system, which will throw up every obstacle it can to keep you in the familiar pain of self-abandonment.

The grief of becoming visible

I didn't expect the grief. Nobody warned me about the grief.

When I started choosing myself, I expected to feel empowered. I expected to feel strong. I expected to feel free. Instead, I felt like I was dying. Because in a way, I was. The version of me that existed to serve everyone else was dying. And that version, as painful as it was, was all I had ever known.

There is a grief in realizing how much of your life you spent not being yourself. There is a grief in looking back at all the relationships where you gave and gave and received nothing in return. There is a grief in realizing that many of the people who claimed to love you only loved the version of you that was small and compliant.

Janina Fisher, a leading expert in trauma treatment, talks about how survivors often have a "survival self" that developed to cope with an unsafe environment. That survival self is not your true self. But it's the self that kept you alive. And when you start to let it go, you will grieve it. You will feel lost. You will wonder who you even are without this role of the caretaker, the people-pleaser, the one who holds everything together.

I remember sitting in my car after a therapy session, sobbing, thinking, "If I'm not the person who takes care of everyone, then who am I?"

And that's the question, isn't it? Who are you when you stop performing for everyone else's approval?

I didn't have an answer. I still don't have a complete answer. But I'm finding it. Slowly. In the small moments where I say no to something I don't want to do. In the moments where I state my preference even if it's inconvenient for someone else. In the moments where I let myself rest even though there are a thousand things I could be doing for other people.

A practical guide to working with your nervous system is Anchored (paid link) by Deb Dana. She translates Polyvagal Theory into actual practices you can use when your system is screaming at you to go back to the familiar pain of self-abandonment. It's helped me stay grounded when the grief gets overwhelming.

Notice what just happened in your body when I said that. The grief is real. And it's okay to feel it. It's okay to mourn the life you didn't get to live because you were too busy taking care of everyone else. It's okay to be angry about it. It's okay to be sad about it. It's okay to feel all of it.

The people who won't survive your growth

Here's the part nobody wants to talk about. When you start choosing yourself, some people in your life will not be happy about it.

Some of them will be confused. Some of them will be angry. Some of them will accuse you of changing, of becoming selfish, of not being the person they thought you were. And they will be right. You are changing. You are becoming someone who doesn't exist to serve them anymore. And that is threatening to a system that depended on your self-abandonment.

I lost friends when I started setting boundaries. I lost the approval of family members who were used to me being the one who absorbed their emotional dysregulation. I lost the identity of being the "good one," the "easy one," the one who never caused any trouble.

And honestly? It was terrifying. For a while, I wondered if I was doing something wrong. Maybe I was being too harsh. Maybe I was being too rigid. Maybe I should just go back to the way things were, because at least then I wasn't alone.

But I couldn't go back. Once you start to feel what it's like to be yourself, even a little bit, the old way of living becomes unbearable. It's like tasting clean water after a lifetime of drinking from a polluted well. You can't un-taste it.

A compassionate reframing of trauma that resonates with many is What Happened to You? (paid link) by Bruce Perry and Oprah Winfrey. It reframes the question from "What's wrong with you?" to "What happened to you?" which is exactly the shift we need when we're judging ourselves for losing relationships that were built on our own self-abandonment.

Here's what I've learned. The people who truly love you will eventually adjust. They might struggle. They might need time. But if they genuinely care about you, they will find a way to be in relationship with the real you. And the people who can't? They were never in relationship with you in the first place. They were in relationship with the version of you that served their needs.

That's a hard truth. Sit with it.

The practice of returning to yourself

Choosing yourself is not a one-time decision. It is a practice. It is something you will have to do over and over again, sometimes every hour, sometimes every minute.

It is asking yourself, before you say yes, "Do I actually want to do this?" And then listening to the answer, even if the answer is no.

It is noticing when your body tightens, when your stomach clenches, when your throat closes, and recognizing that as information. Your body is telling you something. Listen to it.

It is saying, "I need a minute," when someone is pressuring you for an answer. It is saying, "I can't do that right now," when someone asks for your help. It is saying, "That doesn't work for me," when someone proposes something that drains you.

It is letting people be disappointed in you. It is letting people be angry with you. It is letting people leave you. And surviving all of it.

I won't lie to you. Some days, it still feels impossible. Some days, I still default to the old patterns. Some days, I still catch myself contorting into a shape that will make someone else comfortable. And on those days, I try to have compassion for myself instead of shame. Because shame keeps me stuck. Compassion helps me try again.

A gentle introduction to parts work that many people appreciate is No Bad Parts (paid link) by Richard Schwartz. It helps me understand that the part of me that wants to abandon myself isn't bad. It's a part that learned a long time ago that this was the safest way to survive. And that part needs my compassion, not my rejection.

I don't have a neat conclusion for you. I don't have a five-step plan that will make this easy. What I have is this: your needs matter. Your wants matter. Your voice matters. You have a right to take up space in this world. And the practice of choosing yourself, as painful and messy and grief-filled as it is, is the practice of coming home to yourself.

You've been away for a long time. It's okay to be disoriented. It's okay to not know who you are yet. You'll find out. One small choice at a time.

Healing Journey Resources

  • Complex PTSD: From Surviving to Thriving (paid link)
  • Anchored (paid link)
  • What Happened to You? (paid link)
  • No Bad Parts (paid link)
